coredata.dc:title: An electrochemical dopamine sensor based on CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Suqin
Jiang, Renzhong
Liu, Zhuyu
Zhu, Chengcheng
Ma, Zhihui
Pan, Cong
Dai, Gaopeng
coredata.dc:description: CoMn2O4, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometry was employed to characterize its structure and morphology. An electrochemical sensor based on modification carbon paste electrode (CPE) using CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites was developed for sensing dopamine (DA). It demonstrated excellent response ability and electrocatalytic activity for DA detection by cyclic voltammograms (CVs) and i–t plots. Experimental conditions such as CoMn2O4 to CQDs mass ratio, the pH of the electrolyte solution, scan rate, and instrumental parameters for i–t test was optimized. Under optimized conditions, CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) exhibited a remarkable linear relationship between the oxidation current and DA concentration across the ranges of 2.0–1000.0 μM and 1000.0–60000.0 μM in 0.1 M PBS. Additionally, the detection limit was as low as 0.0874 μM. The sensor also expressed good selectivity, reproducibility, stability and recovery between 98.5 % and 104.2 % (RSD <5 %), thereby laying a robust foundation for future practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Central nervous system
Neurochemicals
Electrochemical Sensors
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CoMn2O4, CQDs and CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites
Electrochemical behavior of electrodes with different modifications
Effect of CoMn2O4 to CQDs mass ratio
Detection of DA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices
China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51508172 , 51572076 and 51378183 ), China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation and Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices ( HLOM231003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) X-ray diffraction spectrum and (b) FT-IR spectrum (c) UV–vis and (d) photoluminescence of CQDs, CoMn2O4 and CoMn2O4/CQDs composite with a mass ratio of 1:1.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of different modified electrodes. 1. Bare CPE; 2. CoMn2O4/CPE; 3. CQDs/CPE and 4. CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1). (b) Cyclic voltammograms of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) without and with 400 μM DA.

(a) XRD spectra of CoMn2O4/CQDs complexes with different mass ratios; (b) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon paste electrodes modified with CoMn2O4/CQDs complexes with different mass ratios, inset was the details of curve b and d; (c) Cyclic voltammograms of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) in PBS solutions with different pH; (d) Cyclic voltammograms of CoMn2O4/CQDs/ CPE (1:1) cyclic voltammograms at different sweep speeds; (e) AC impedance spectra of different modified electrodes; (f) i-t plots of constant potential electrolysis of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) at different potentials.
(a) Response i ∼ t curves of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) with continuous addition of different concentrations of DA; (b) linear curves of DA concentration versus oxidation peak current.
Schematic view of the electrochemical redox behavior of DA on the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE.
Comparison of the electrochemical performances used for DA detection.
Determination results of DA in human urine.

head.ce:title: An electrochemical dopamine sensor based on CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposite modified carbon paste electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering Hubei University of Arts and Science China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering
Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Materials and Devices China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering
Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CoMn2O4, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometry was employed to characterize its structure and morphology. An electrochemical sensor based on modification carbon paste electrode (CPE) using CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites was developed for sensing dopamine (DA). It demonstrated excellent response ability and electrocatalytic activity for DA detection by cyclic voltammograms (CVs) and i–t plots. Experimental conditions such as CoMn2O4 to CQDs mass ratio, the pH of the electrolyte solution, scan rate, and instrumental parameters for i–t test was optimized. Under optimized conditions, CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) exhibited a remarkable linear relationship between the oxidation current and DA concentration across the ranges of 2.0–1000.0 μM and 1000.0–60000.0 μM in 0.1 M PBS. Additionally, the detection limit was as low as 0.0874 μM. The sensor also expressed good selectivity, reproducibility, stability and recovery between 98.5 % and 104.2 % (RSD <5 %), thereby laying a robust foundation for future practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: DA is an important small-molecule neurotransmitter widely found in the nervous system, playing a crucial role in regulating diverse physiological functions such as mood, motivation, and movement [1–4]. Abnormal fluctuations in DA concentration have been closely associated with various neuropsychiatric disorders, including Parkinson's disease, depression, and schizophrenia [5,6]. Therefore, the development of highly sensitive and selective assays for DA is of significant clinical importance for the early diagnosis of these conditions and the real-time monitoring of therapeutic effects. Traditional DA assays include gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [7], high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [8], high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [10], electrochemiluminescence [11], and spectrophotometry [12]. However, these methods either involve time-consuming pre-treatment, complex experimental conditions [13,14], or expensive and large-scale instrumentation, which limits their widespread application.

Electrochemical methods have gradually become a prominent approach in the field of DA detection due to their ease of operation, exceptional sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness [15]. However, despite the considerable potential demonstrated by electrochemical sensors in DA detection, they continue to face numerous technical challenges in further enhancing their selectivity and sensitivity when analyzing complex biological samples.

According to the studies, carbon paste electrodes (CPEs) first proposed reported by Adams in 1958 [16] by thoroughly mixed carbon powder (such as glassy carbon, graphite, & other carbonaceous materials) and a proper water-immiscible or non-conducting binder. CPEs can be highly applied in electrochemical and electroanalysis studies, as a result of the merits like very low background currents, lower costs, convenient preparation, simplified surface renewal processes, large potential window, simple miniaturization, and feasible incorporation of various materials in the course of the paste preparation [17–20]. Although conventional carbon paste electrodes have demonstrated commendable sensitivity and selectivity, their electrocatalytic performance still requires improvement. As a result, the functionalization and modification of carbon paste electrodes to enhance their electrocatalytic performance has become a key focus in contemporary electrochemical sensor research. CPEs are particularly notable for their wide range of applications in the electrochemical detection of DA by chemically functionalization and modification. A modified graphene paste electrode (MCPE) incorporating CuCo2O4 bimetallic oxide and its composite with graphene quantum dots for dopamine detection was carried out by Madadi [21]. The developed GQD/CuCo2O4/CPE sensor exhibited a lower detection limit (0.004 μM) with the linear range from 0.5 to 150 μM. An innovative, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly electrochemical sensor, based on a carbon paste electrode modified byshrimpshell waste (CPE-SS) was developed to facilitate the electrocatalytic detection of DA. The CPE-SS electrode provide a wide linear range of 10–1000 μM, and low detection limit 3.6 µM [22].

In recent years, carbon-based materials have emerged as a research hotspot for electrochemical sensors, they play an important role as modifier material of working electrode. Carbon-based materials, such asporous carbon,carbon quantum dots,graphene oxides,graphitic carbon nitrides,carbon nanotubes, etc. have been found in sensor fabrication [23]. They provide largesurface areaand active sites for adsorption and which in turn improve the sensitivity for the detection of analytes.CQDs, the intensifying nanostructured form of carbon material, have exhibited incredible impetus in several research fields such as bio-imaging [24], bio-sensing [25], drug delivery systems, optoelectronics andphotocatalysis [26], thanks to their extensive photonic and electronic properties, as well as their light-collecting, tunablephotoluminescence and so on [27]. A steady rise in publications pertaining to the utilization of CQDs for dopamine detection, particularly from 2015 onwards, focusing on three facets: electrochemical, fluorescent, and colorimetric sensors [28].Furthermore, CQDs are frequently used with other materials to formulate high-performance CQDs composites, enhancing the efficiency of dopamine detection for rapid, sensitive, and highly specific outcomes.

Among various modification materials, transition metal oxide [29] or metal sulfides [30] nanoparticlesof Iron, Titanium, Manganese,Zirconium, Cobalt, Nickel, Cadmium and their composites in the field of electrochemical and photoelectrochemical sensing [31–33] have garnered significant attention for their favorable electrocatalytic performance and chemical stability. CoMn2O4, as a transition metal oxide, with the cobalt and manganese atoms forming strong bonds with oxygen atoms that facilitate rapid electron transfer. It acted as an attractive candidate for constructing electrode material in terms of its good cycling performance, outstanding physicochemical stability, high electrical conductivity and low toxicity. Moreover, it was applied in the fields such as supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, electrochemical detection and oxygen evaluation reaction and so forth [34]. The catalytic performance of CoMn2O4 can be optimized by modulating its lattice structure [35]. Additionally, the established preparation methods and relatively low production costs of CoMn2O4 make it suitable for large-scale industrial applications. These outstanding properties make CoMn2O4 an ideal choice for enhancing the performance of electrochemical sensors. However, to date, there has been minimal research dedicated to the modification of electrodes with CoMn2O4 for the sensitive detection DA.

Based on the aforementioned context, this paper introduces a novel method to modify carbon paste electrodes by employing a nanocomposite of CoMn2O4 and CQDs, with the objective of providing an innovative solution for the electrocatalytic detection of DA through optimizing these composites. Initially, we synthesized CoMn2O4/CQDs composites via a straightforward hydrothermal synthesis process and subsequently modified them onto the surfaces of carbon paste electrodes. Our research systematically evaluated the electrochemical properties of the composite electrode, encompassing electrocatalytic activity, selectivity, and stability, thereby exploring its potential application in the electrochemical detection of DA. We anticipate unveiling the application prospects of this novel electrode and offering fresh insights and ideas for the design of high-performance electrochemical sensors.

Cobalt nitrate, manganese sulfate, citric acid, vitamin B1, coumarin, thiourea and other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. The reagents used in the experiments were analytically pure, and the water used in the experiments was ultrapure water. Electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Co., China); FI-IR (IRPrestige-21, SHIMADZU); S-4800 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan); X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS Co., Germany). Three-electrode system: A bare or modified carbon paste electrode (3 mm in diameter), saturated calomel electrode, and platinum wire electrode were employed as working, reference, and auxiliary electrodes, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CoMn2O4, CQDs and CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CoMn2O4 was synthesized by hydrothermal method [36]: 0.5 mM of cobalt nitrate, 1 mM of manganese sulfate, and 30 mM of urea were weighed and dissolved in a mixture of 40 mL (1:1 vol ratio) of secondary deionized water and ethanol. After magnetic stirring for 20 min, it was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 10 h. Centrifugation was performed at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was washed with ultrapure water and ethanol alternately for several times, dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h, and cauterized at 450 °C for 1 h in a muffle furnace to obtain the product.

CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method: 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of urea was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 6 h, cooled down to room temperature and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed in dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1 kDa) for 72 h. The supernatant was then freeze-dried for further use.

CoMn2O4/CQDs nanocomposites were synthesized by hydrothermal method: 0.5 mM of cobalt nitrate, 1 mM of manganese sulfate and 30 mM of urea were weighed and dissolved in a mixture of 40 mL (v/v 1:1) of secondary deionized water and ethanol, and carbon quantum dots with a mass ratio of 1:1 with cobalt manganate were added. It was magnetically stirred for 20 min, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 10 h. Centrifugation was performed at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the precipitate was washed with ethanol for several times to obtain a brown precipitate, which was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the complex of cobalt manganate and carbon quantum dots with a mass ratio of 1:1. The complexes with mass ratio of 1:2, 1:3, 2:1 and 3:1 were prepared in the same way.

100 mg of graphite powder, and 50 μL of paraffin oil was drawn into a quartz mortar using a micropipette. The mixture was manually ground for approximately 30 min until a homogeneous paste was achieved. Bare carbon paste electrodes were created by filling this paste into electrode cavities of 3 mm diameter, compacting, and connecting with copper wires. A total of eight types of electrodes were produced: CPE, CoMn2O4/CPE, CQDs/CPE, and CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE with different mass ratios (1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 2:1, 3:1). The graphite powder and paraffin oil employed for these electrodes maintained the same weight ratio, with 14.3 mg of cobalt manganate, carbon quantum dots, and different cobalt manganate to carbon quantum dot complexes being incorporated. Electrode surfaces were polished with weighing paper and rinsed three times with deionized water after each polishing session for experimentation.

The X-ray diffraction patterns of CQDs, CoMn2O4 particles and CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) composites are presented in Fig. 1 a. A broad peak at about 25° is observed in the XRD pattern of CQDs, corresponding to the (0 0 2) diffraction of low-graphitized carbonaceous structure. As depicted, the diffraction peaks of CoMn2O4 are situated at 29.28°, 33.34°, 36.44°, 60.69°, and 64.60°, corresponding to the (2 0 2), (1 1 3), (3 1 1), (2 2 4), and (4 0 0) crystal planes of the spinel CoMn2O4, respectively (JCPDS No. 77-0471) [36,37]. Additionally, the figure reveals stronger diffraction peaks at 24.71°, 32.58°, and 38.57°, which align with the (0 1 2), (1 0 4), and (1 1 0) crystal planes of the metal carbonate. This suggests the presence of a precursor to spinel CoMn2O4, specifically MnCO3 formed via urea hydrolysis (JCPDS No. 85-1109) and CoCO3 (JCPDS No. 78-0209) [38]. The XRD spectra of the CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) composites closely resembled that of CoMn2O4, with no discernible diffraction peaks for CQD. This absence may be due to the small size or amorphous nature of CQDs, rendering their diffraction signals inconspicuous in the plot. Fig. 1b showcases the FT-IR spectra of CQDs within the wave number range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. Notably, an evident –OH absorption peak at 3200 cm−1, a weak absorption peak near 1730 cm−1 indicative of C=O stretching, an absorption peak at 1380 cm−1 due to C–O stretching, and a characteristic peak at 1221 cm−1 for the non-conjugated stretching vibration of C–N are observed. These functional groups enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of CQDs in aqueous systems. A comparison with the infrared spectra of CQDs prepared as per literature reveals that the primary functional group absorption peaks are essentially identical [39]. Consequently, based on the aforementioned analyses, it is confirmed that the samples prepared in this experiment are indeed CQDs. For spinel CoMn2O4, the transmittance peak obtained at 557 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibration from the metal–oxygen environment in the octahedral site, and the peak at 628 cm−1 is related to the metal–oxygen stretching vibration from the tetrahedral site. The peak observed around 1601 cm−1 is related to the stretching mode vibration of the H–O–H molecule and the peak located at 3405 cm−1 is due to H–O–H bending mode vibration [40]. It is clear that the synthesized CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) is close agreed with CoMn2O4 FTIR spectrum, and also no other peaks about CQDs were observed.

In Fig. 1c–d, the UV–Visible absorption spectra and emission behavior of CQDs and CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) samples were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1c, the CQDs had a characteristic peak about 329 nm. In the presence of CoMn2O4, no shift in wavelength was observed in the absorption spectrum, but the peak decreases significantly. The emission behavior was analyzed by PL at three different excitation wavelengths, i.e. 350, 450 and 550 nm. As it can be seen, the emission behavior of CQDs is dependent to the excitation wavelength, a red shift in the resulted emission spectra. Which is probably due to the irradiative electron recombination as a result of distribution of particle size [41]. When CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) is excited at 350 nm, no fluorescence generation is observed. It is possible that the CQDs enter the lattice of CoMn2O4 during the synthesis process, resulting in no fluorescence emission, which is consistent with the results obtained by FTIR and UV–Vis. Moreover, the size and shape of CoMn2O4/CQDs were analyzed using HRTEM (as shown in Fig. S1). The obtained TEM images showed that the CQDs had an average size of about 10 nm, were spherical and well dispersed in solution.

As demonstrated in Fig. S2, scanning electron microscope (SEM) images at both low and high magnifications of CoMn2O4 particles along with CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) composites are presented. As observed in Fig. S2a, the CoMn2O4 particles synthesized individually display an irregular microsphere morphology with a rough surface. Conversely, the CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) complex shown in Fig. S2b predominantly exhibits a homogeneous microsphere shape with a relatively smooth surface. This observation can be attributed to the solvation effect of urea, which facilitates the formation of homogeneous precipitates. Fig. S2c-d respectively illustrate the surface morphologies of CoMn2O4 microsphere particles and CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) complex microsphere particles under high magnification. In Fig. S2c, it is evident that the CoMn2O4 microsphere particles consist of irregular bulk polyhedra and nanoparticle aggregates, featuring numerous tiny pores with inconsistent shapes and sizes distributed on the surface, resulting in an irregular microsphere appearance. In contrast, as depicted in Fig. S2d, the CoMn2O4/CQDs (1:1) composite microsphere particles are formed by aggregate structures of uniformly sized and regularly shaped triangular stars, rendering the surface of the composite microsphere particles smoother and more uniform in morphology. As a result, this greatly increases the specific surface area of the composite, which increases from 68.9 m2/g to 97.5 m2/g for the modified material (Fig. S3). In addition, XPS analysis was also employed to discuss the surface elemental composition of CQDs and CoMn2O4/CQDs. As depicted in Fig. S4a and Fig. S4c, the single spectrum of carbon is divided into three peaks corresponding to C–C, C–O, and C=O bonds (Fig. S4b and Fig. S4d). The three characteristic peaks in the single spectrum of oxygen correspond to the three different forms of oxygen (Fig. S4b and Fig. S4d). Different valence states of the elements Co and Mn were observed in the CoMn2O4/CQDs composite samples (Fig. S4e–f) [35–38], and therefore, the XPS results indicate the successful preparation of CQDs and CoMn2O4/CQDs samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of electrodes with different modifications
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviors of CPE, CoMn2O4/CPE, CQDs/CPE, and CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE composite-modified electrodes towards DA were examined in Fig. 2 a at a concentration of 400 μM, with a supporting electrolyte of pH 7.0 PBS solution. As depicted in curve 1, no discernible redox peaks were observed on the CPE. Curve 2 indicates that CoMn2O4/CPE also exhibited no significant redox peaks, likely due to the weak adhesion between graphite powder and CoMn2O4, resulting in poor cycling performance for CoMn2O4/CPE. Curve 3 reveals a subtle change on CQDs/CPE, possibly attributable to the abundant carboxyl groups on the CQDs surface, yet no prominent redox peaks were detected. Curve 4 illustrates that a pair of distinct redox peaks emerged on the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE composite-modified electrode. By comparing curves b and d, it is evident that the presence of CQDs not only acts as a carbon-based material offering substantial attachment area but also enhances the stability of the composite-modified electrode, thereby facilitating the electrochemical reaction. Additionally, the synergistic interaction between CoMn2O4 and CQDs significantly accelerates the electrode reaction. Fig. 2b illustrates the electrochemical behaviors of the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE composite-modified electrodes in the absence and presence of DA. In the absence of DA, a pair of distinct redox peaks appeared at 0.036 V and 0.258 V. According to literature [42], the electrode surface undergoes a phase transition between CoCO3 and Co(OH)2, with the reduction peaks at 0.036 V arising from the CoO2/CoOOH electrode pair reactions: Co(OH)2 + OH− ⇔ CoOOH + H2O + e− and CoOOH + OH− ⇔ CoO2 + H2O + e−. The oxidation peak at 0.258 V stems from the Mn–O/Mn–O–OH electrode pair reaction. Upon the addition of DA, the intensities of the oxidation and reduction peaks markedly increased, with slight broadening of the peak widths, signifying the electrocatalytic oxidation of DA on the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE composite-modified electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CoMn2O4 to CQDs mass ratio
ce:section.ce:para: Different mass ratios of CoMn2O4 to CQDs not only influence the morphology of the composites but also significantly impact their electrocatalytic and electrical conductivity properties. The XRD patterns of CoMn2O4/CQDs complexes with different mass ratios were meticulously examined, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. The pristine CoMn2O4 exhibited peak splitting at the primary diffraction angles of 24.71°, 32.58°, and 38.57°. Upon the introduction of CQDs, and with composite ratios of 3:1 and 2:1, the splitting phenomenon progressively diminished; when the ratio was adjusted to 1:1, the splitting entirely vanished, manifesting a solitary main peak with the maximum diffraction intensity. This behavior can be attributed to the hydration of CQDs, facilitating the thorough integration of the two components and enhancing the crystalline structure of CoMn2O4, thereby optimizing its crystallinity. Continuing to augment the mass of CQDs resulted in weakened diffraction peaks at ratios of 1:2 and 1:3, albeit without the occurrence of splitting. This structural variation was corroborated through cyclic voltammetry, as presented in Fig. 3b. The electrochemical behavior of DA was scrutinized across different mass ratios of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE nanocomposite-modified electrodes operating with a DA concentration of 400 μM and a supporting electrolyte of pH 7.0 PBS solution. The findings demonstrated that, with increasing CQDs mass, no discernible redox peaks were observed at ratios of 3:1 and 2:1; however, upon reaching a ratio of 1:1, prominent redox peaks emerged. When the CoMn2O4/CQDs mass ratio escalated to 1:2, the redox peaks exhibited marked diminution and deteriorated peak morphology, while at a ratio of 1:3, the redox peaks entirely vanished. This observation underscores that the incorporation of CQDs substantially augments the conductivity of CoMn2O4-modified carbon paste electrodes, particularly when the mass ratio of CoMn2O4/CQDs composite is optimized at 1:1, thereby rendering the electrochemical performance most efficient and expediting the electron transfer rate.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the electrochemical response of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) composite-modified electrodes to 0.4 m M DA was investigated in PBS solutions at various pH values (pH = 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, and 9.0) with a sweep rate of 20 mV/s and a potential range of −0.2 to 0.5 V. The results reveal that the oxidation peak current reached its maximum at pH 7.0, demonstrating that the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) modified electrode exhibits the most significant electrocatalytic performance for DA in neutral PBS (pH 7.0). Additionally, as the solution pH increased, both the oxidation peak potential (E pa) and the reduction peak potential (E pc) gradually decreased, indicating the involvement of protons in this electrode reaction. According to Fig. S5a, both E pa and E pc showed a strong linear relationship with pH, represented by the linear equations: E pa = −0.03057pH + 0.5747 (n = 6, R2 = 0.9398) and E pc = −0.02671pH + 0.2161 (n = 6, R2 = 0.9799). The slopes of these equations were found to be 30.57 mV/pH and 26.71 mV/pH, respectively, which were close to half of the theoretical value of 59 mV/pH for the Nernst equation at 25 °C [43], demonstrating that the redox reaction of DA on the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) was a reversible electron transfer process coupled with an equivalent amount of proton transfer to protons [44].

As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the influence of sweep rate on the electrode performance was investigated across a sweep rate range of 10 to 300 mV/s. The sequence of sweep rates was as follows: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, and 300 mV/s. With the progressive increase in sweep rate, the oxidation peak currents and reduction peak currents of the modified electrode increased correspondingly, accompanied by an increase in the peak potential difference. A good linear correlation between the peak currents and the sweep rate was observed (Fig. S5b), with the linear equations being I pa (μA) = 0.1631v + 11.43, R2 = 0.9784, and I pc (μA) = −0.2893v − 21.77, R2 = 0.9961. These linear relationships indicated that the redox process of the modified electrode was governed by surface adsorption.

Fig. S5c shows that ln v has a good linear relationship with E pa and E pc (10–300 mV/s), respectively. The linear equation was E pa = 0.04933 ln v + 0.4805 (R2 = 0.9934) and E pc = −0.02481 ln v −0.08616 (R2 = 0.9876). According to the Laviron equation [45]:
(1)
E pa
-
E 0
=
RT ln v
/
1 - α
nF
(2)
E pc
-
E 0
=
RT ln v
/ α nF

The electron transfer coefficient (α) and the number of electrons transferred (n) can be calculated as 0.67 and 1.57 (≈ 2), respectively. This result proves that the redox process of DA involves two protons and two electrons [46].

As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results for bare CPE, CoMn2O4/CPE, CQDs/CPE and CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) are presented. The measurements were conducted in 0.1 M KCl solution using 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 as the chemical probe, with the frequency range extending from 0.1 Hz to 500 Hz. The diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot is indicative of the interfacial electron transfer resistance. The order of the semicircle diameters is: bare CPE > CoMn2O4/CPE > CQDs/CPE > CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1). This suggests that the addition of appropriate quantities of CQDs and CoMn2O4 can enhance the electrical conductivity of the carbon paste electrodes, with the optimal conductivity achieved at a 1:1 ratio of CoMn2O4 to CQDs. The improvement in conductivity at the optimal ratio is likely due to the increased specific surface area of the electrode, which facilitates electron transfer across the electrode surface. The impedance value of the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) nanocomposite-modified electrode is notably low, approximately in the range of a few tens of ohms, confirming its superior conductivity and further validating the conclusions derived from Fig. 3e.

The applied potential is a significant factor influencing current response. As depicted in Fig. 3f, this experiment utilized the current–time (i-t) method to examine the response of CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) composite-modified electrodes to the oxidative peak current of DA across various applied potentials (0.1 V (a), 0.15 V (b), 0.2 V (c), 0.25 V (d), 0.35 V (f), and 0.4 V (e)). Under continuous homogeneous stirring, 4 μL of 0.1 M DA was introduced into 10 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) solution at 60 s intervals. As evidenced by the i-t curves, the response current progressively augmented with escalating DA concentration, and the time required to attain 95 % of the maximum response current typically did not exceed 5 s. As the applied potential increased, the enhancement in DA's response current steadily grew; the maximum enhancement was observed at a constant potential of 0.35 V (f), but this trend reversed when the applied potential was further increased to 0.4 V. Consequently, 0.35 V was deemed the optimal potential for DA detection.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, 0.1 M DA standard solution was continuously introduced into a uniformly stirred 0.1 M PBS electrolyte solution at intervals of 60 s. Simultaneously, the i ∼ t curves of the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) were recorded, with the results presented in Fig. 4 a. The i ∼ t curves reveal that the oxidation peak current response progressively intensified with increasing DA concentration. The DA concentration exhibited a favorable linear relationship with the oxidation peak current signal across both the 2.0–1000 μM and 1000 ∼ 60,000 μM ranges. Specifically, within the 2.0–1000 μM concentration range, the curve relating the oxidation peak current to concentration was I pa = −1.963–0.04774C, yielding a correlation coefficient of 0.9904. In contrast, within the 1000 ∼ 60,000 μM concentration range, the curve was I pa = −48.84–0.00199C, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9967, and its linear relationship is depicted in Fig. 4b. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated according to the 3σ/s rule (σ is the standard deviation of the blank, and s denotes the slope of the standard curve) was as low as 0.0874 μM. In comparison to other electrochemical sensors reported in the literature for DA detection (as summarized in Table 1 ), the sensor designed in this study not only boasts a lower detection limit but also offers a broader linear range.

Based on the above experimental results, we propose an electrochemical redox mechanism of DA on CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) (Fig. 5 ). CoMn2O4 is a bimetallic oxide with excellent electrochemical properties, while CQDs provide good electronic conductivity and chemical stability. The specific process is as follows, (i) Initial adsorption: DA is first adsorbed on the CoMn2O4/CQDs composite surface of the modified electrode. Due to the large surface area of CQDs and the abundant –OH and –OH2 groups presence, DA molecules from the solution can be stably adsorbed on the surface of the composite material. (ii) Electron transfer: Under the effect of electrode voltage, rendering the modification film redox-active and thus boosting the resulting current response for DA oxidation. Specifically, the hydrogen atoms on the amino group and benzene ring of DA are oxidized to produce dopamine cation intermediates. In this process, electrons are transferred from DA to the conduction band of the CoMn2O4/CQDs composites. CoMn2O4 acts as an active site, which facilitates the efficient transfer of electrons. (iii) Generation of intermediates: the generated dopamine cations continue to undergo further oxidation on the electrode surface, which ultimately generates dopaquinone (DQ), a two-electron transfer process. (iv) Electron reduction: under certain conditions, DQ can be reduced to its original form by the electrode voltage, and the DQ can be reduced to its original form by the electrode voltage. Under certain conditions, DQ can be reduced back to DA, which is a reverse electrochemical process in which electrons from the conduction band of the CoMn2O4/CQDs composite return to DQ, reducing it to DA [46]. (v) Role of the composites: Throughout the redox process, the role of CoMn2O4 is to provide a highly efficient electron-transfer pathway, whereas the CQDs enhance the material's electron conduction and the surface chemical stability, allowing the redox process of DA to proceed. stability of the material, making the redox process of DA more efficient and controllable. The synergistic effect of CoMn2O4 and CQDs allows this process to be carried out efficiently on the electrode surface.

In the electrochemical detection of DA using a CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1), other electroactive substances may affect the current response; therefore, an interference test was performed. As illustrated from a to f in Fig. S6a, interfering substances with concentrations ten times that of DA were added, including melamine, sodium sulfate, urea, vitamin B1, coumarin, and thiourea, while the concentration of DA was 60 μM. The results indicated that, within the permissible relative error of ±5 %, the change in current response was minimal after the addition of these interfering substances, suggesting that they did not interfere with the determination of DA. Thus, the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) exhibits excellent selectivity for DA detection. Under the optimized conditions, six CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) were prepared separately using the same method, and the current response for a 400 μM DA solution was determined, as shown in Fig. S6b, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.4 %. One of the prepared CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) was selected and stored in a refrigerator, with peak currents measured at 1 h intervals. The experimental results demonstrated that the peak current remained relatively stable, exhibiting an RSD of 0.92 % over 10 h (Fig. S6c), indicating that the modified electrode possesses good reproducibility and stability, making it suitable for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the applicability of the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) composite modified electrode for real-world DA detection, three urine samples from healthy individuals were randomly selected. Each 100 μL of the urine samples were pipetted into separate 2.0 mL centrifuge tubes, to which 20 μL of DA standard solutions at varying concentrations were added. The mixtures were then vortexed and thoroughly agitated before being centrifuged for 10 min. The supernatant was collected, filtered through a membrane, and subsequently analyzed using the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) to measure the peak current. The DA content in each sample was calculated using the working curve, and the recovery rate was determined. Each sample was analyzed in triplicate, with the average values presented in Table 2 . The recovery rate of DA ranged from 98.5 % to 104.2 %, indicating satisfactory results. Thus, the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) composite modified electrode shows great potential for practical DA detection in real-world samples.

In this study, CoMn2O4 and CQDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) were then fabricated by uniformly mixing these materials with graphite powder and paraffin oil in a specific ratio. The study systematically investigated the influence of various parameters, including the mass ratio of CoMn2O4 to CQDs, electrolyte pH, scan rate, and applied potential, to determine the optimal reaction conditions. The resulting composite-modified electrode exhibited a highly efficient electrocatalytic response to DA, enabling its quantitative detection. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the CoMn2O4/CQDs/CPE (1:1) demonstrated a wide linear detection range for DA, from 2.0 to 60000.0 μM, with an impressively low detection limit of 0.0874 μM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Furthermore, the sensor displayed excellent selectivity, stability, and reproducibility, making it well-suited for the analysis of real samples with highly satisfactory results, thereby showcasing significant potential for practical applications.

Suqin Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Renzhong Jiang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhuyu Liu: Validation, Software, Methodology. Chengcheng Zhu: Software, Methodology. Zhihui Ma: Methodology. Cong Pan: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Gaopeng Dai: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51508172, 51572076 and 51378183), China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation and Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices (HLOM231003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112889.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
